title: My Pro-Life-to-Abolitionist Conversion
author: Michael Lockwood
orientation: A first-person testimony from a former pro-lifer who walks through why he now rejects incremental legislation in favor of state nullification of Roe v. Wade and the immediate, total criminalization of abortion. Lockwood argues incrementalism is ineffective, dishonest, and morally compromised because it codifies the legality of murdering some classes of preborn children.
audiences: An incrementalist legislator or policy advocate
A Christian wrestling with biblical duty to civil authority
An abolitionist looking to persuade pro-life friends

questions.question: Why does Lockwood believe waiting on the Supreme Court to overturn Roe v. Wade is futile?
questions.answer: He argues only Clarence Thomas is a confirmed vote to overturn Roe, while at least five justices won't even uphold modest regulations like a hospital admittance law. Because the court rules by precedent rather than the Constitution, he sees no realistic path to overturning Roe within our lifetimes.
questions.quote: There is virtually no hope of the supreme court overturning Roe within our lifetimes.

questions.question: What is the constitutional basis Lockwood gives for states defying the Supreme Court on abortion?
questions.answer: He appeals to Article VI's Supremacy Clause and the 14th Amendment's due process and equal protection guarantees. Because the Constitution itself prohibits depriving any person of life without due process, he concludes states are obligated to obey the Constitution rather than an unconstitutional ruling from the court.
questions.quote: In other words, the Constitution prohibits abortion. Therefore, since the supreme court said abortion is a right, the supreme court must be disobeyed.

questions.question: Which Bible passages does Lockwood cite to support state defiance of the Supreme Court?
questions.audience: A Christian wrestling with biblical duty to civil authority
questions.answer: He cites Proverbs 24:11 (rescue those being taken to death), Amos 5:15 (establish justice in the gate), and Acts 5:29 (we must obey God rather than men). He treats the application to unborn babies and to defying the court as self-evident.
questions.quote: Acts 5:29 — "We must obey God rather than men [the supreme court]."

questions.question: How effective does Lockwood say the 20-week ban actually is at saving babies?
questions.audience: An incrementalist legislator or policy advocate
questions.answer: He cites CDC data that 98.7% of abortions occur before 20 weeks, meaning even a maximally effective 20-week ban could save at most 1.3% of aborted babies in a state. He calls this no real victory because it allows nearly the same number of killings to continue.
questions.quote: So, even if somehow every mother who would have killed her baby after 20 weeks forgot to hurry up and do it before she reached the point of it becoming illegal, the ban would only save 1.3% of babies who were aborted in that State.

questions.question: Why does Lockwood claim incremental laws actually delay abolition rather than advance it?
questions.answer: He argues that any pro-life-majority legislature already has the power to abolish abortion via nullification, so when those legislators settle for incremental bills they are substituting them for full abolition. By accepting partial measures, pro-life lawmakers fail to protect every baby they could be protecting.
questions.quote: By substituting abolition with incremental legislation, pro-life legislators aren't "at least saving some babies." They're failing to protect all babies in their State even though they have to power to do so.

questions.question: What concrete example does Lockwood give of pro-life legislators blocking abolition?
questions.audience: An abolitionist looking to persuade pro-life friends
questions.answer: He points to Oklahoma, where a Republican supermajority repeatedly kills proposed abolition bills and replaces them with incremental ones to placate pro-life voters. He calls this behavior cowardly and singles out Oklahomans for Life as actively lobbying against abolition.
questions.quote: Every time, the bill gets killed, and all the pro-life legislators replace it with an incremental bill that they pass in order to appease their pro-life constituents. It's wrong and it's cowardly.

questions.question: What does Lockwood mean when he calls incremental legislation "dishonest"?
questions.audience: An incrementalist legislator or policy advocate
questions.answer: He says heartbeat bills require legislators to argue from the false premise that a heartbeat is what gives a baby value, and hospital-admittance laws require pretending abortion is healthcare. The strategic framing spreads dehumanizing misinformation about preborn humans and never names abortion as murder.
questions.quote: In a weird strategic effort to save babies by making it hard for clinics to operate, pro-life legislators have to lie and pretend that abortion is healthcare. That's dishonest.

questions.question: What is Lockwood's primary moral objection to incremental abortion bills?
questions.audience: A Christian wrestling with biblical duty to civil authority
questions.answer: He argues that any law protecting only certain classes of preborn babies necessarily codifies into law that it is permissible to murder the rest. A heartbeat law sanctions killing babies without heartbeats; a 20-week ban sanctions killing babies under 20 weeks.
questions.quote: The primary reason that incremental legislation is immoral is that it sanctions murder.

questions.question: How does Lockwood connect incremental legislation to the 14th Amendment?
questions.answer: He argues that protecting only some babies from murder establishes unequal protection under the law. Because the 14th Amendment requires equal protection, any incremental law that draws lines between classes of preborn humans is itself unconstitutional.
questions.quote: A law which establishes that some babies should be protected from murder and others should not establishes UNEQUAL protection. An incremental law violates the Constitution!

questions.question: Why does Lockwood say "it's better than nothing" reasoning is moral relativism?
questions.audience: A Christian wrestling with biblical duty to civil authority
questions.answer: If goodness is measured against the prior status quo rather than against God's standard, then a 24-week ban becomes "moral" in California (where abortion is wider) but "immoral" in Oklahoma (where a 20-week ban already exists). He insists God's standard of justice is consistent and absolute, not contextual.
questions.quote: With this reasoning, passing a law codifying the legality of murdering a baby up to 24-weeks and making it illegal after that would be moral in California, because it's better than what California had before, but immoral in Oklahoma, where there's already a 20-week ban, so a 24-week ban would expand abortions.

questions.question: What does Lockwood identify as the faulty presupposition that kept him stuck in pro-life thinking?
questions.answer: He used to measure laws against the baseline of abortion being totally legal, treating any reduction as good. He now argues the correct measuring stick is total illegality, so anything short of complete abolition is immoral because it codifies the permissibility of killing some children.
questions.quote: The measuring stick for goodness is abortion being completely ILLEGAL. That means that anything LESS than complete abolition of abortion is immoral, because it codifies into law the permissibility of murdering some children.

questions.question: How does Lockwood explain the abrasiveness pro-lifers often perceive in abolitionists?
questions.audience: An abolitionist looking to persuade pro-life friends
questions.answer: He acknowledges that pride sometimes drives it, which is sinful, but argues that much of what looks like abrasiveness is rebuke of pro-lifers who have been called to repent and have refused. He says advocating for faulty legislation that delays abolition merits rebuke.
questions.quote: Many times, however, the abolitionist has already called the pro-lifer to change their ways several times, and the pro-lifer still refuses to do so. So, the abolitionist rebukes them. I think this is appropriate.

questions.question: What does Lockwood predict would happen if every pro-lifer became an abolitionist?
questions.audience: An abolitionist looking to persuade pro-life friends
questions.answer: He projects that abortion would be abolished in roughly half the country in the very next legislative session. He frames the persistence of legal abortion as primarily a problem of pro-life resistance, not pro-choice power.
questions.quote: If every pro-lifer became an abolitionist today, then next legislative session, abortion would be abolished in half the country.

questions.question: Why does Lockwood call Oklahomans for Life an "enemy" of the abolition movement?
questions.audience: An incrementalist legislator or policy advocate
questions.answer: He says that in states like Oklahoma where Planned Parenthood and Democrats hold no power, the only obstacle to immediate abolition is the pro-life establishment itself, which has repeatedly lobbied against the abolition bill. He insists the line between true allies and pretended allies must be drawn.
questions.quote: Get that drilled into your head. Oklahomans for Life is actually our enemy. They are our ENEMY.

related.title: Immediate, Not Gradual Abolition of Abortion
related.reason: Companion piece making the core abolitionist argument against gradualism that Lockwood adopts here.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Expands the case against heartbeat bills, 20-week bans, and other incremental legislation Lockwood critiques.

related.title: WATCH: How the Seven-Pronged Pro-Life Political Strategy Has Failed
related.reason: Documents the strategic failure of the pro-life movement that Lockwood describes as a 'hopeless marathon on a hamster wheel.'

related.title: Why Do Pro-Lifers Despise Abolitionists?
related.reason: Addresses the same tension Lockwood identifies between pro-lifers and abolitionists, including perceptions of abrasiveness.

related.title: Are Nullification's Advocates the "Villains" of American History?
related.reason: Defends the constitutional doctrine of state nullification that Lockwood proposes as the path to abolishing abortion.

related.title: Nullification
related.reason: Movement reference page on the Article VI / Supremacy Clause argument Lockwood invokes against Roe.

related.title: Abolitionist, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: Articulates the same identity distinction Lockwood says he is writing his testimony to draw.
